title: Key Concepts: Serfdom
description: Let's dig into the history of unfree agriculture in post-Carolingian Europe.
topic_path: European Middle Ages: feudalism and serfdom

assessment_items.question_content:
The *demesne* of a manorial estate is

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the farmland of the lord, worked by serf and later hired labor
the tenant farmer working the land
the extended family of the lord owning the manor

hints: While knights were vassals, they weren't the only kind of vassal.

choices.content: someone who pledges allegiance to a superior feudal power, usually in exchange for land
a knight who leaves his hometown to fight for the preservation of Christianity
a landowner who rents out his estate to peasants so they can work the land

assessment_items.question_content:
Roman legal reforms in the third century CE led to _coloni_ status
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The legal reforms did not make labor practices more equitable by modern standards.

choices.content: becoming hereditary, passed from parent to child.
expanding to include anyone who worked on land.
being recognized as a form of slavery.

assessment_items.question_content:
_Formariage_ or _merchet_ is the practice of

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Serfs weren't free, but neither could they be purchased.

choices.content: a peasant paying a fee to a lord before receiving permission to marry outside the lord's land.
a lord giving a peasant a pass to leave the estate for a designated period of time.
a lord purchasing another lord’s workforce of peasants.

assessment_items.question_content:
The collapse of the Carolingian empire in the late 9th century CE led to
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Byzantium wouldn't fall to the Turkish Ottoman Empire until 1453.

choices.content: A fracturing of Western Europe into many kingdoms and duchies.
the emergence of a strong centralized government with the Franks.
the takeover of Turkish forces from the east.
